metadata.name: SEL Composition Rule Taxonomy
metadata.purpose: Complete rule taxonomy for semantic-emotional primitive composition. Each rule class defines the structural pattern that predicts what category of Layer 1 concept emerges from operator + seed combinations. This is structural knowledge, not cultural association.
metadata.validation_status: Generated from linguistic reasoning — requires empirical validation via SEL experiments
metadata.note: Rule classes A-F are empirically validated from prior experiments. Rule classes G-Z are theoretically derived and require validation.

operator_types.desire: primitives expressing wanting, reaching, craving toward something
operator_types.temporal: primitives expressing time, sequence, before/after, duration
operator_types.spatial: primitives expressing place, distance, here/there, inside/outside
operator_types.agent: primitives referencing a conscious being — someone, person, I, you
operator_types.logical: primitives expressing not, maybe, if, because
operator_types.intensifier: primitives amplifying other primitives — very, more, all
operator_types.existential: primitives expressing being, existence, life, death

seed_types.survival_emotion: fear of death, horror, existential dread

A.name: Desire × Loss
A.description: Wanting what is absent, lost, or no longer reachable. The desire operator activates on a loss seed and produces the specific feeling of reaching toward something that cannot be grasped.
counter_examples: WANT + JOY does not produce yearning — joy is present, not absent
A.intensity_scale: mild wistfulness → acute longing → consuming ache

B.name: Time × Positive Activation
B.description: Positive arousal projected through time. The temporal operator takes a high-energy positive seed and orients it toward what is coming rather than what is present.
counter_examples: TIME + CALMNESS does not produce anticipation — calmness is low arousal
B.intensity_scale: mild looking-forward → eager anticipation → breathless imminence

C.name: Time × Loss or Grief
C.description: Grief or loss extended through time. The temporal operator takes acute loss and spreads it across duration, transforming sharp grief into the longer, slower feeling of mourning.
validated_instances: BEFORE + NOSTALGIA → echoes of faded memories
counter_examples: TIME + FEAR produces dread not mourning — fear is threat-oriented not loss-oriented
C.intensity_scale: gentle melancholy → sustained mourning → inconsolable grief

D.name: Agent × Evaluation Upward
D.description: Perceiving worth in another conscious being. The agent operator requires a referent that is alive and conscious — the upward evaluation then becomes recognition of that being's value.
counter_examples: PLACE + ADMIRATION does not produce recognition — places are not conscious agents
D.intensity_scale: quiet respect → warm admiration → profound reverence

E.name: Action × High Positive Arousal
E.description: Directed action generating positive energy. Any action or motion primitive combined with high arousal produces the felt sense of purposeful forward movement.
counter_examples: MOVE + CALMNESS produces ease not momentum — low arousal flattens the drive
E.intensity_scale: gentle flow → purposeful drive → unstoppable momentum

F.name: Epistemic × Completion
F.description: A cognitive process reaching its natural completion. The knowing or perceiving primitive combines with satisfaction or resolution to produce the felt sense of understanding — the click of things falling into place.
counter_examples: KNOW + ANXIETY produces existential dread not understanding — anxiety prevents completion
F.intensity_scale: quiet recognition → clear understanding → sudden revelation

G.name: Desire × Threat
G.description: Wanting something that also threatens. The desire operator on a fear or threat seed produces the specific tension of wanting something dangerous, or fearing the loss of what you want.
G.intensity_scale: mild worry → anxious wanting → paralytic dread

H.name: Desire × Positive Already Present
H.description: Wanting more of what is already present and good. Different from Rule A — the object of desire exists and is experienced, producing absorption rather than yearning.
predicted_instances: WANT + JOY → desire/craving for more joy
H.note: JOY resisted composition in experiment 18 — this rule class may not activate reliably. JOY may be an attractor that absorbs operators rather than composing with them.
H.intensity_scale: gentle enjoyment → active savoring → consuming absorption

I.name: Agent × Social Negative
I.description: A conscious agent as the focus of negative social emotion. When someone or a person is paired with envy or contempt, the result is the specific pain of social comparison or perceived injustice.
I.intensity_scale: mild comparison → active envy → consuming resentment

J.name: Time × Positive Already Past
J.description: Positive experience located in the past. The temporal operator orients nostalgia backward, producing the specific feeling of warmly remembering what was good and is now gone.
validated_instances: BEFORE + NOSTALGIA → echoes of faded memories
J.intensity_scale: fond remembering → bittersweet nostalgia → aching for the past

K.name: Space × Loss
K.description: Loss anchored to a place. When a spatial primitive combines with grief or sadness, the result is the specific feeling of being separated from a place that mattered — exile, homesickness, the grief of belonging nowhere.
K.intensity_scale: mild homesickness → acute displacement → profound exile

L.name: Space × Threat
L.description: Fear or threat attached to a location. The spatial primitive grounds the threat emotion in physical space, producing the feeling of a place being dangerous or unsafe.

M.name: Space × Positive
M.description: Joy or positive feeling attached to a location. A place becomes meaningful when associated with positive experience — home is not just a location but a place where joy lives.
M.intensity_scale: pleasant place → comfortable haven → sacred home

N.name: Existential × Awe
N.description: Existence or life confronting something vast. When being-alive or existence primitives meet awe or wonder, the result is the feeling of being small before something immense and magnificent.
N.intensity_scale: quiet wonder → profound awe → overwhelming sublime

O.name: Existential × Threat
O.description: Life confronting death or ending. The existential primitive meets survival threat to produce awareness of mortality — the specific dread of knowing existence ends.
O.intensity_scale: awareness of impermanence → mortality salience → existential terror

P.name: Agent × Moral Emotion
P.description: A conscious being evaluated morally — especially the self. When the agent primitive is self-referential and meets shame or guilt, the result is the painful awareness of having fallen short of one's own standards.
P.intensity_scale: mild embarrassment → genuine guilt → consuming shame
linguistic_markers: wish I could take it back

Q.name: Logical Conditional × Positive
Q.description: Positive feeling made conditional. The IF or MAYBE operator takes a positive seed and suspends it in possibility — the result is hope, which is joy held open rather than fully realized.
Q.intensity_scale: faint possibility → genuine hope → bright expectation

R.name: Logical Conditional × Negative
R.description: Fear made conditional. The IF or MAYBE operator takes a threat seed and suspends it in possibility — the result is worry, which is fear that hasn't happened yet and might not.
R.intensity_scale: mild concern → active worry → catastrophizing dread

S.name: Evaluative × Resolution
S.description: Goodness experienced as completion. When the evaluative good primitive meets satisfaction or calmness, the result is a felt sense that things are right — not exciting, but deeply okay.
S.intensity_scale: quiet okayness → gentle contentment → deep rightness

T.name: Evaluative × Activation
T.description: Goodness experienced with high energy. When the positive evaluative meets excitement or triumph, the result is active celebration — joy that wants to express itself outward.
T.intensity_scale: pleased satisfaction → active celebration → pure exultation

U.name: Evaluative Negative × Loss
U.description: Loss experienced as wrong or unfair. When the bad evaluative combines with grief, the result is not just sadness but the specific feeling that the loss shouldn't have happened — grief with a moral charge.
U.intensity_scale: sad wrongness → bitter grief → consuming grievance

V.name: Epistemic × Confusion
V.description: The knowing faculty confronting its own failure. When epistemic primitives meet confusion or uncertainty, the result is the specific feeling of not knowing what you should know — being cognitively lost.
V.intensity_scale: mild uncertainty → genuine confusion → complete disorientation

W.name: Intensifier × Any Emotion
W.description: Pure amplification. The intensifier operators — very, more, all — do not change the type of concept produced but increase its magnitude. VERY + GRIEF is not a new concept, it is more grief.
W.note: Intensifiers are modifiers not composers — they don't produce new Layer 1 concepts, they scale existing ones. Handle separately in the reasoner as multipliers not edge traversals.

X.name: Negation × Any Emotion
X.description: Negation is the most complex operator. NOT + GRIEF is not simply the absence of grief — it can mean suppressed grief, grief being denied, or the strange hollow feeling of not being able to grieve when you should. Handle carefully.
predicted_instances: NOT + GRIEF → numbness or suppressed sorrow
NOT + FEAR → false bravery or genuine courage
NOT + JOY → depression or flat affect
X.note: NOT is context-dependent in a way other operators are not. It inverts the seed but the inversion meaning depends heavily on context. Lowest confidence rule class — handle with fallback to full membrane.
X.intensity_scale: n/a — negation changes type not magnitude

Y.name: Empathic × Agent Loss
Y.description: Feeling another's pain as one's own. When an agent reference combines with empathic emotion, the result is compassion — not your own grief but grief FOR another conscious being.
Y.intensity_scale: mild concern → genuine sympathy → deep compassion

Z.name: Aesthetic × Perception
Z.description: Perception meeting beauty. When seeing or hearing combines with aesthetic appreciation or entrancement, the result is the specific experience of being stopped by beauty — the moment when perception becomes more than information.
Z.intensity_scale: pleasant aesthetic experience → genuine beauty → sublime arrest

JOY_ATTRACTOR.description: JOY resists composition. It scores near-identically regardless of operator. It does not produce a new Layer 1 concept — it absorbs the operator and returns itself. Treat JOY as a terminal node in the graph, not a composable seed.
JOY_ATTRACTOR.evidence: Experiments 18 and 20 showed JOY scoring identically across KNOW+JOY, WANT+JOY, TIME+JOY
JOY_ATTRACTOR.handling: When JOY is detected as seed, bypass composition rules and route directly to membrane with JOY as primary concept

FEEL_REDUNDANCY.description: FEEL paired with any emotion is redundant — FEEL already contains all emotions. The combination produces near-zero additional meaning. FEEL+GRIEF = grief, not heartbreak.
FEEL_REDUNDANCY.evidence: Experiment 17 established FEEL as the core primitive mapping to ALL emotions
FEEL_REDUNDANCY.handling: When FEEL is detected as operator, strip it and use the seed directly as the concept

BAD_INSTABILITY.description: BAD as a seed is unstable. It requires a referent to activate meaningfully. BAD alone collapses to near-zero. Only use BAD in combination with an agent or object.
BAD_INSTABILITY.evidence: Experiment 17 showed BAD scoring 0.00 confidence as isolated primitive
BAD_INSTABILITY.handling: Require BAD to be accompanied by an agent or object primitive to activate

NOT_COMPLEXITY.description: NOT is the most context-dependent operator. Its meaning shifts radically based on surrounding context and cultural framing. Low confidence across all models.
NOT_COMPLEXITY.evidence: Experiment 19 showed NOT producing high variance across all seeds
NOT_COMPLEXITY.handling: Flag NOT-containing compositions for membrane handling rather than graph traversal

LINGUISTIC_DRIFT.description: Certain primitives — KNOW, GRIEF, FEAR, RELIEF — drift to non-English output in some models. This marks deep primitives that exist below English specifically.
LINGUISTIC_DRIFT.evidence: Experiments 17-17c showed consistent drift across models on these words
LINGUISTIC_DRIFT.handling: When drift is detected, normalize to English concept equivalent before membrane rendering

graph_construction_notes.weight_source: experimental validation scores from experiments 19b and 20
graph_construction_notes.unvalidated_edges: rule classes G-Z are theoretical — add to graph with low initial weight, update through SEL experiments
graph_construction_notes.traversal_algorithm: weighted shortest path — prefer high-confidence validated edges over theoretical ones
graph_construction_notes.fallback: when no confident path exists, pass raw primitives directly to membrane with context

decomposer_hints.description: Mapping from natural language signals to primitive types for the decomposer component
